Re:
Farn PolrrrcAt- PRACTTcEs Corr,rvrssroN
428 J Street . Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA g5g14-232g
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916.t 322-0886
January 13,20ll
Gilbert J. Granito
Redwine and Sherrill
1950 Market Street
Riverside, California 92501- 17 ZO
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 4-10-187
Dear Mr. Granito:
This letter responds to your reqlrest for advice on behalf of the Coachella Valley Water
District' the Colorado River Joint Powers Authority, and Gerald Zimmerman regarding the post
governmental employment provisions of the political Reform Act (the,.Act,,).r
QT,IESTIONS
l' will the former Executive Director of the Colorado River Board, Gerald Zimmerman,
who has taken a new undesignated position with the Colorado River Board, be subject to an
additional one-year revolving door ban after he leaves his employment in the undesignated
position?
2' May Gerald Zimmetman work in an undesignated position at the Colorado River
Board and also work as an independent contractor for ihe colbrado River Joint powers Authority
simultaneously if he does not engage in any activities that are prohibited by the Act,s one-year
revolving door ban?
CONCLUSIONS
l' No. If Gerald Zimmerman works in a position with the Colorado River Board that is
not and should not be designated in the agency's ionflicrof-[nterest code, he will not be further
restricted by the Act's one-year revolving cloor ban as o ,"r,-,lt of having worked in that position.
' The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections g1000 through gl0l4. All statutory
ret'erences are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair political practices
Commission are contained in sections l8l l0 through 18997 of Title 2 t>f tieCalitbrnia code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Califbrnia Code of Regulations, unless orherwise indicated.


File No. A-10-187
Page No. 2
2' Yes, Gerald Zimmerman may work in an undesignated position at the Colorado River
Board and as an independent contractor with the Colorado River Joint powers Authority
simultaneously. He may not engage in any activities that are prohibited by the Act,s one-year
revolving door ban or Section 87100 while working at the Colorado River Joint powers
Authority.
FACTS
You are the General Counsel for the Coachella Valley Water District (..CVWD,,). The
cvwD is a member of the colorado River Joint Powers Autiiority ("CRJpA';;, *hi"h consists
exclusively of six local member agencies. All of the CRJPA agencies have a representative seat
on the Colorado River Board of Califomia's ("CRB") l0-person goveming board. The CRB is a
state agency. The CVWD, CRJPA and the CRB entered into a Joint poweis Agreement, dated
May 13, 7947, for the purpose of jointly sponsoring, encouraging and supporti;g a separate,
independent unincorporated association of citizensif Southern Califomia referred to as the
Colorado River Association. You have been authorized by the CVWD, CRJPA and Gerald
Zimmerman to seek advice on their behalf.
Gerald zimmerman is the former Executive Director of the CRB. Mr. Zimmerman left
his position as Executive Director on November 10, z)l},and is now working at the CRB in an
undesignated position as a retired annuitant. You stated that Mr. Zimmermanls a well respected
expert on Colorado River issues. ln Mr. Zimmerman's new position his primary purpose is to
assist the CRB in upcoming negotiations with other Colorado River Basin states and Mexico.
You state that this undesignated position will not entail Mr. Zimmerman making or participating
in any decisions that-may foreseeably have a material effect on his financial interests. Also,
Mr' Zimmerman will not be involved in negotiating or signing any contract awarded through
competitive bidding, in making decisions in conjunction with-the competitive bidding process, or
in negotiating, signing, or making decisions on contracts executed pursuant to section 10122 of
the Public Contract Code.
You ask whether the CRB's employment of Mr. Zimmerman in a non-designated
position starts the clock running for the Act's one-year revolving door prohibition, so that after
working in this non-designated positiolfo^r at leasi one year, the-Act's tne-year ban period will
have run and Mr. zimmerman may wolk {ol another "niity, 
such as the CVWD, and appear
before the CRB on the CVWD's behalf without the restrictions imposed by the Act,s one-year
ban.
You also ask whether Mr. Zimmerman may work concurrently at the CRB in his current
position and atdhe CRJPA as an independent contiactor for the primary purp; of researching
and compiling the history concerning the evolution and development or ilrri"sources of the
Colorado River. Mr. Zimmerman would be asked to research and prepar" popo, for the
CRBJPA that would include the following areas in which Mr. Zimmeirnor'*u, involved during
his career at CRB:


File No. A-10-187
Page No. 3
o Development of California's Colorado River Water Use Plan (the 4.4plan) and the
Quantification S ettlement Agreem ent (2002).
o Development of the Interim Surplus Guidelines (2001).
o Lower colorado River Multi-species conservation plan (2005).
- 
These papers will only address issues resolved prior to zOOG,not any currently under
discussion by the CRB. During the one-year prohibition period, Mr. Zimmerman,s work will be
reviewed only by his supervisor at the CRJPA, Tom Levy, in order to ensure the material is not
used to influence any current CRB discussions. Mr. Zimmernan may also be asked to research
and prepare papers in areas in which he was not involved, includin gih",l.rizona v. Califurnta
lawsuit, the six Party Priority Agreement and the colorado River Compact.
a
ANALYSIS
Public officials are subject to three types of post-governmental employment restrictions
under the Act.
First, there are two provisions that apply before leaving office:
The Act restricts public officials ability to influencing prospective employment by
prohibiting a public employee from participating in, oiusing his or t ei oiriciat position to
influence, a governmental decision directly relating to any person with whom he or she is
negotiating, or has an affangement conceming, proipective employment (Section g7407,
Regulation 18747 ).
Section 87100 which prohibits public officials at any level of state or local government from
making, participating in making, or in any way attempting to use his or her official position
to influence a governmental decision in which he or she k]ro*, or has reason to know he or
she has a financial interest. (section g7100; Regulation 1s700.)
Once an official leaves office, the following apply:
A one-year "revolving door" restriction bars certain state employees from communicating,
for compensation, with their former agencies for the purpose of influencing certain
administrative or legislative action. (section gT4o6,itegulation 1g746.1.)
A permanent ban prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides" to participate, for
compensation, in a proceeding involving the State of Califomia if ti'e proceeding is one in
which the former stallemployee participated while employed by the siate. (Sections g7401-
87 402, Regulation 187 41.1.)
a
a
a


File No. A-10-187
Page No. 4
The Ban Against Influencing prospective Employment
The Act t::]ti:tt influencing prospective employment while employed with a public
agency. This prohibition is found at Section g7407;
"No public official shall make, participate in making, or use his or her official
position to influence, any governmental decision diiectly relating to any person
with whom he or she is negotiating, or has any arrangement concerning,
prospective employment.,'
The term'public official" is defined, in part, in Section B204gas ... . . every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or locai govemment agency, but does not include
judges and court commissioners in the judicial brilch of government.,, As an employee at the
CRB, Mr. Zimmerman would be subject to this ban.
"A public official is 'negotiating' employment when he or she interviews or discusses an
offer of employment with an [potentialf empioyer or his or her agent.,, (Regularion ft7a7@)(l).)
The commission has construed the schiduling, conduct, and foll-ow-up io ai interview as one
continuous process falling under the definition of "negotiating" employm "ni. lBonner Advice
Letter' No' I-98-287') However, the mere act of sendi-ng a resume or application to a potential
employer has not been considered "negotiating." similirly, entertaining informal inquiries about
future plans and accepting expressionJor g"rJ.ul in;;r;;, discussing E1npt,oy-".r,
opportunities at some point in the ftilure iJnot considered "negotiatin g.,, (1d..) ..A public official
has an 'arrangement'-concerning prosp_ective employment when he oishe actepts an employer,s
offer of employmenr.', (Regula tion lg7 47(cX2). j
- 
Under your facts, it appears that there is an arrangement for Mr. Zimmerman to work for
cvwD' Thus, this prohibition would apply and Mr. Ziimerman would be prohibited from
Ttilg,participatingin making, or using hls official position to influenc", ;, governmental
decision directly relating to CVWD.
In addition, as noted above, Section 87100 applies to all public officials and is not limited
to those designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code. we have enclosed a factsheet on
87100 for your information.
Finally, you also asked whether Mr. zimmernan, while working in the nondesignated
position at the cRB, could also work as an independent contractor for the cRJpA. The Act
ffi',fl,[iffIil:,;J*:]fi,jffiH:emprovment, 
,o rong as h; J;;';;;.*"+ in anv acrivitv
The One-Year,.Revolving Door,, Restriction
The one-year prohibition applies to employees who were designated or should have been
designated in their former ug.n"y'iionflicrof-interest code. Accordingly, the one-year ban still


File No. A-10-187
Page No. 5
applies if a position should have been designated in an agency's conflict-of-interest code,
because the individual in the position made or participateA initre making of governmental
decisions that may have had a reasonably foresieable material effect o, or"irnis or her
financial interests. (section 87406(dX1); Regulati on rg746.1(a)(4),copy enclosed.)
A state employee "makes a governmental decision" when, acting within the scope of his
or her authority, he or she: 1) votes on a matter; 2) appoints a person; 3iobligates his or her
agency to any course of action; 4) enters into any contractual agreement on bihalf of his or her
agency; or 5) determines not to act, unless the determination ishade due to a conflict of interest.
(Regulation 187 02.l (copy enclosed).)
A state employee "participates in making a govemmental decision" when he or she
negotiates (without significant substantive review) with a governmental entity or private person
regarding a governmental decision, or when he or she advises or makes recommendations to the
decisionmaker (either directly or without significant substantive review), by conducting research
or an investigation or by presenting any report, analysis or opinion that iequires the exercise of
independent judgment on the part of the employee and the outcome or purpose of which is to
influence the decision. (Regulation l87OZ.2 (copy enclosed).)
We have advised that an employee participates in the making of a governmental decision,
even if it is reviewed by several of his or her supeiiors, if any of tnJfottoviing apply: l) the
superiors rely on the data or analysis prepared by the employee without checting-it
independently;2) the superiors rely on the professional judgment of the employee; or 3) the
employee in some other way actually influences the final dJcision. (Lilyquiitedui"" Letter, No.
M-96-318 [Memorandum to the Attomey General].)
You have stated that Mr. Zimmermanis now in an undesignated position in which he is
not required by the CRB to file a statement of economic interests. However, from the facts
provided in your request for advice, it appears that his new position is one that should be
designated in his agency's conflict-of-interest code because Mr. Zimmerman would be assisting
with negotiations between the CRB and other Colorado River Basin states and Mexico by
conducting research and likely making recommendations to leaders of his agency while serving
as an expert on issues involving the Colorado River. Therefore, even if Mr.-Zimmerman,s
current position is not included in the CRB's conflict-of-interest code, pursuant to the
requirements of Section 87302, the one-year ban period applicable to Mr. Zimmerman and his
involvement before the cRB has most likely not begun to run because it appears he continues to
hold a pos^ition at the CRB that entails the making oi participation in the making of governmental
decisions.2 (Section 87406(dXl); Regulatio n 18746.1(a)(2); (Jnterreiner Advice Letter, No. I-9g-
299.) 
I
2 You should contact your code reviewing body or the Commission's Technical Assistance Division to
assist you in determining whether Mr. Zimmerman is property included in or excluded fiom the agency,s contlict of
interest code. Determination of this issue will also determine what Mr. Zimmerman,s ,"rponrilirities are with
respect to filing Form 700s.


File No. A-10-187
Page No. 6
If Mr' Zimmerman is placed in a position at CRB that does not involve any of the above
described actions, and thus should not be designated in the CRB conflict-of-interest code, the
one-year period would begin once he left his designated position and would not be extended as a
result of having served in the non-designated posiiion.
section 87406 prohibits specified officials from acting as agents or attomeys or otherwise
representing, for compensation, "any other person, by making ony lormal or informal
appearance, or by making any oral or written communication, beiore any state administrative
agency, or officer or employee thereof," for one year after the official leit the agency,s
employment "if the appearance or communicatiin is made for the purpose of inJlttencing
adrninistrative or legislative action, or inflttencing any action or proceeding involving the
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocatton oj o plrmit, license, grant, or contract, or the
sale.or purchase of goodl or propertyi' (Emptrasis aaoeo.j rn" ooJ-y"* period for the ban
begins to run once the official has left his designated position with the agency.
Regulation 187 46.2(a) further provides :
"(a) For purposes of Govemment Code Section 87406,a formal or informal
appearance or oral or written communication is for the pu{pose of influencing if it
is made for the principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing,
modifying, opposing, deraying, or advincing the action oiproceedid:l"
appearance or communication includes, but is not limited to, converJing by
telephone or in pers-on, coresponding with in writing or by electronic
transmission, attending a meeting, and delivering oriending any communication.,,
Regulation 18746.1(b) outlines the circumstances when the prohibitions of the one-year
ban will apply' under this regulation, an official covered by the one-year ban is prohibited from
making an appearance or communication if all of the foilowing apply:
"(1) The official has left his or her state office or employment, which means he or
the ha,s either permanently reft state service or is o., u l.ur" of absence.
"(2)The appearance or cofitmunication is made within 12 months after leaving
state office or employment.
"(3) The public official is compensated, or promised compensation, for the
appearance or communication. However, a payment made for necessary travel,
meals, and accommodations received directiyin connection with voluniary
services is not prohibited or limited by this slction.
"(4) The appearance or communication is made on behalf of any person as an
agent, attomey, or representative of that person. An appearance or communication
made by a public officiar solely to represent his or hei personal interests, as
defined in2 cal. code Regs., Section rg702.4, subdivision (bxl), is not
prohibited or limited by this section.


File No. A-10-187
Page No. 7
"(5) The appearance or communication is made for the purpose of influencing, as
defined in2 Cal. Code Regs. Section 18746.2, any legisiative or administrative
action, or any discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or
revocation of a permit, license, grant or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods
or property.
"(A) Services performed to administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements of
an existing P,ermig license, grant, contract, or sale agreement may be excluded
from the prohibitions of this regulation, provided the services do not involve the
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of any of these actions or
proceedings . . ..
"(6) The appearance or communication is made before any officer or employee of
any of the following:
"(A) Any state administrative agency that the public official worked for or
represented during the 12 months before leaving state office or employment . . ..
"(B) Any state administrative agency which budget, personnel, und oti".
operations are subject to the direction and control of any agency described in
subdivision (bX6XA) . . ..
"(C) Any state administrative agency subject to the direction and control of the
Govemor, if the official was a designated employee of the Governor's office
during the Lz months before leaving state office tr employment.,, 3
Communications restricted by the one-year ban include any formal or informal
appearance or oral or written communication made to influence legislative or administrative
action or any action on a proceeding. (Section 87406(d)(l).) These communications include,
but are not limited to, conversing directly or by telephone,'corresponding by writing or e-mail,
attending a meeting, and delivering or sending uny "o-*unication. 6elguiation lg746.Z(a).)
A communication is considered to be for the purpose of influencing legiJative or administrative
action "if it is made for the principal purpose of iupporting, pro*oiingl influencing, modifying,
opposing, delaying, or advancing the action or proieedin*"4 lReguliiion 1g746.2(a).)
Certain communications are not rcstricted under the one-year ban. A communication is
not subject to the one-year ban when the former official:
3 Regulation I 8746' I (b)( I ) refers to an official leaving a state position that is designated in the agency,s
confl ict-of-interest code.
a "Legislative action" is defined at Section 82Q37 tomean "the drafting, introduction, consideration, r
modification, enactment or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other matter by the
Legislature or by either house or any committee, subcommittee, jointor select committee thereof, or by a member or
employee of the Legislature acting in his official capacity. "Legislative action" also means the action of the
Governor in approving or vetoing any bill." Section gZmztr,) -provides 
that ..'Administrative action, means the
proposal, drafting, development, consideration, amendment, enictment, or defeat by any state agency of any rule,
regulation, or other action in any ratemaking proceeding or any quasi-legislatir" pro...airg, *rii.r, shall include any
proceeding governed by Chapter 3.5 (commencing with- Section i t:+o)'of part I of Division 3 of Title 2.


File No. A-10-187
Page No. 8
"(1) Participates as a panelist or formal speaker at a conference or similar public
event for educational purposes or to disseminate research and the subject matter
does not pertain to a specific action or proceeding;
"(2) Attends a general informational meeting, seminar, or similar event;
"(3) Requests information concerning any matter of public record; or
"(4) Communicates with the press." (Regulation rg746.2(b)(l)-(4).)
Thus, the one-year ban would not prevent Mr. Zimmerman from requesting information
generally available to the public about agency business from the CRB or otirer state agencies
while working at the CRJPA. Nor would it prohibit him from attending iJo.-otioral meetings
regarding existing laws, regulations, or polic^ies, so long as he does ,ot"att"*ft to influence
legislative or administrative actions by ihe cRB. Mr. Zimmerman would noibe prohibited from
having social conversations with "-pioy.., of the cRB that are not aimed at influencing its
administrative or legislative actions ire not prohibited by the ban. (Tobias Advice Letter, No.
A-96-089.) (Section 87406(dXl).)
Whether a particular meeting or conversation is for the purpose of influencing
administrative or legislative action or a.specific proceeding (as defined by Regul ation 1g746.2)
depends on the facts of each case. For instanc",lf u, ex-designated employee attends a public
meeting with many other persons, where- there- are many topics on the agenda, it may be
reasonable to infer that the ex-designated employ""', uit"ndance is not ior the purpose of
influencing the agency's action. conversely, *h"r" there is a small meeting to discuss a
particular administrative or legislative action, or _other specific action o. pro-""."oing involving the
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of u p".-it, license, grant, or contract, or the sale
or purchase of goods or property (section 87406(d)i it may more readily be inferred that the
former designated employee's presence at the -""tirg is intended to influence agency action.
(Ramirez Advice Letter, No. 4-99-300.)5
Similarly, as noted ab9ve, Regulation 18746.1(b)(5)(A) provides that performing services
to administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements oi an existing permit, license, grant,
contract, or sale agreement are not considered appearances or communications prohibited under
Section 87406, so long as Mr. zimmerman does not influence rril;;;i;riJr" 
or legislarive
action, or the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, Iicense, grant, or contract,
or the sale or purchase of goods or property.
I
Accordingly, for purposes of the one-year ban under Section g74}6,the Act does not
prevent Mr' Zimmerman from accepting post-govemmental employment in the private sector or
a local government agency such as trre cvwo, but for twelve months after he leaves a
5 Further information is provided in the enclosed fact sheet prepared by the Commission to address
common questions associated with post_employment restrictions.


File No. A-10-187
Page No. 9
designated position or position that should be designated at the cRB, he may not make any
appearance before his former employer for the pu.por" of influencing administrative or
Iegislative action, or.influencing any action or iroceeding involving the issuance, amendment,
awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or coitract, or the sale or purchase of goods
or property.
The Permanent Ban on.,switching SidesD
The Act's permanent.oan prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides,, and
participating, for compensation, in a specific proceeding involving the State of Califomia, or
assisting others in the proceeding, if the proceeding is o-ne in which the former state employee
participated while employed by the stare. (See secJions g74ol-g7402; Regulation 1g741.1.)
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban applicable to any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other
proceeding in which Mr. Zimmerman participatea while empl,oyed ur.u rtut.-udministrative
official' A State administrative official--.*r o'every member, 6ffi""., "*pioy"" of a state
administrative agency who as part of his or her official responsibilities engages in any judicial,
quasi-judicial or oth^er ptoceedings in other than a purely clerical, secretarial or ministerial
capacity." (Section 87400(b).)
"'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding, application, request
for a ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge,
accusation, arrest or other particular matter involving a specifil purty oipurties in any court or
state administrarive agency. . .." (Section g7400(c)J en official has ..p;;;i;ated,,in a 
..
proceeding if he or she took part in the proceeding ;'personally, and substantia'ily through
decision, approval, disapproval, formal written reiomm"ndation, renaerinfaovice on a
substantial basis, investigation, or use of confidential information . . ..,, (Section g7400(d).)
The permanent ban does not apply to a "new" proceeding even in cases where the new
proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior pro"""ding in which the official had participated.
A "new" proceeding not subjectto the permanent ban typlcally involves different parties, subject
matter, or circumstances that differ from those involved i, p..uiou, proceedints. (rtlsl Advice
Letter, No' A-04-187; also see Donovan Advice Letter, No. t-or-t te.; New clntracts let by the
employee's former agency, in which the former employee did not paiicipate, are consioered new
proceedings. (Leslie Advice Letter, No. I-89-649.) A lnew" contract is one based on new
consideration and new terms, even if it involves the same parties. (FerberAdvice Letter, No.
r-99-104: Anderson Advice Letter, No. A-98-159.) In addition, the application, drafting, and the
award of a contract, license, or approval is considered a proceeding seiarate from the monitoring
and performance of the contract, license, or approval. (Anderson, supra; BlonienAdvice Letter,
No. 4-89-463.)
we have outlined the general provisions of the permanent ban for your review. Though
you have stated the general areas in which Mr. zimmerman will be working while at cRJpA,
you have not advised us in detail of Mr. Zimmerman's participation in spec"ific froceedings

while in state service,-nor of the potential.relationship between any such proceedings and matters

assistance in evaluating services rilot-irg to_a specific pioceeding in which he mayhave
previously participated, he should seek iurtheriaui." it en he can provide us with the relevant
facts.
You also asked whether Mt. Zimmernan, while working in a non-designated position at
the cRB, could also work as an independent contractor for the cRJpA. The Act would not

These papers will only address issues resolved prior to zOOG,not any currently under
discussion by the CRB. During the one-year prohibition period, Mr. Zimmerman,s work will be
reviewed only by his supervisor at the CRJPA, Tom Levy, in order to ensure the material is not
used to influence any current CRB discussions. Mr. Zimmernan may also be asked to research
and prepare papers in areas in which he was not involved, includin gih",l.rizona v. Califurnta
lawsuit, the six Party Priority Agreement and the colorado River Compact.

sections.analysis:
Public officials are subject to three types of post-governmental employment restrictions
under the Act.
First, there are two provisions that apply before leaving office:
The Act restricts public officials ability to influencing prospective employment by
prohibiting a public employee from participating in, oiusing his or t ei oiriciat position to
influence, a governmental decision directly relating to any person with whom he or she is
negotiating, or has an affangement conceming, proipective employment (Section g7407,
Regulation 18747 ).
Section 87100 which prohibits public officials at any level of state or local government from
making, participating in making, or in any way attempting to use his or her official position
to influence a governmental decision in which he or she k]ro*, or has reason to know he or
she has a financial interest. (section g7100; Regulation 1s700.)
Once an official leaves office, the following apply:
A one-year "revolving door" restriction bars certain state employees from communicating,
for compensation, with their former agencies for the purpose of influencing certain
administrative or legislative action. (section gT4o6,itegulation 1g746.1.)
A permanent ban prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides" to participate, for
compensation, in a proceeding involving the State of Califomia if ti'e proceeding is one in
which the former stallemployee participated while employed by the siate. (Sections g7401-
87 402, Regulation 187 41.1.)
a
a
a
The Ban Against Influencing prospective Employment
The Act t::]ti:tt influencing prospective employment while employed with a public
agency. This prohibition is found at Section g7407;
"No public official shall make, participate in making, or use his or her official
position to influence, any governmental decision diiectly relating to any person
with whom he or she is negotiating, or has any arrangement concerning,
prospective employment.,'
The term'public official" is defined, in part, in Section B204gas ... . . every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or locai govemment agency, but does not include
judges and court commissioners in the judicial brilch of government.,, As an employee at the
CRB, Mr. Zimmerman would be subject to this ban.
"A public official is 'negotiating' employment when he or she interviews or discusses an
offer of employment with an [potentialf empioyer or his or her agent.,, (Regularion ft7a7@)(l).)
The commission has construed the schiduling, conduct, and foll-ow-up io ai interview as one
continuous process falling under the definition of "negotiating" employm "ni. lBonner Advice
Letter' No' I-98-287') However, the mere act of sendi-ng a resume or application to a potential
employer has not been considered "negotiating." similirly, entertaining informal inquiries about
future plans and accepting expressionJor g"rJ.ul in;;r;;, discussing E1npt,oy-".r,
opportunities at some point in the ftilure iJnot considered "negotiatin g.,, (1d..) ..A public official
has an 'arrangement'-concerning prosp_ective employment when he oishe actepts an employer,s
offer of employmenr.', (Regula tion lg7 47(cX2). j
- 
Under your facts, it appears that there is an arrangement for Mr. Zimmerman to work for
cvwD' Thus, this prohibition would apply and Mr. Ziimerman would be prohibited from
Ttilg,participatingin making, or using hls official position to influenc", ;, governmental
decision directly relating to CVWD.
In addition, as noted above, Section 87100 applies to all public officials and is not limited
to those designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code. we have enclosed a factsheet on
87100 for your information.
Finally, you also asked whether Mr. zimmernan, while working in the nondesignated
position at the cRB, could also work as an independent contractor for the cRJpA. The Act
ffi',fl,[iffIil:,;J*:]fi,jffiH:emprovment, 
,o rong as h; J;;';;;.*"+ in anv acrivitv
The One-Year,.Revolving Door,, Restriction
The one-year prohibition applies to employees who were designated or should have been
designated in their former ug.n"y'iionflicrof-interest code. Accordingly, the one-year ban still
applies if a position should have been designated in an agency's conflict-of-interest code,
because the individual in the position made or participateA initre making of governmental
decisions that may have had a reasonably foresieable material effect o, or"irnis or her
financial interests. (section 87406(dX1); Regulati on rg746.1(a)(4),copy enclosed.)
A state employee "makes a governmental decision" when, acting within the scope of his
or her authority, he or she: 1) votes on a matter; 2) appoints a person; 3iobligates his or her
agency to any course of action; 4) enters into any contractual agreement on bihalf of his or her
agency; or 5) determines not to act, unless the determination ishade due to a conflict of interest.
(Regulation 187 02.l (copy enclosed).)
A state employee "participates in making a govemmental decision" when he or she
negotiates (without significant substantive review) with a governmental entity or private person
regarding a governmental decision, or when he or she advises or makes recommendations to the
decisionmaker (either directly or without significant substantive review), by conducting research
or an investigation or by presenting any report, analysis or opinion that iequires the exercise of
independent judgment on the part of the employee and the outcome or purpose of which is to
influence the decision. (Regulation l87OZ.2 (copy enclosed).)
We have advised that an employee participates in the making of a governmental decision,
even if it is reviewed by several of his or her supeiiors, if any of tnJfottoviing apply: l) the
superiors rely on the data or analysis prepared by the employee without checting-it
independently;2) the superiors rely on the professional judgment of the employee; or 3) the
employee in some other way actually influences the final dJcision. (Lilyquiitedui"" Letter, No.
M-96-318 [Memorandum to the Attomey General].)
You have stated that Mr. Zimmermanis now in an undesignated position in which he is
not required by the CRB to file a statement of economic interests. However, from the facts
provided in your request for advice, it appears that his new position is one that should be
designated in his agency's conflict-of-interest code because Mr. Zimmerman would be assisting
with negotiations between the CRB and other Colorado River Basin states and Mexico by
conducting research and likely making recommendations to leaders of his agency while serving
as an expert on issues involving the Colorado River. Therefore, even if Mr.-Zimmerman,s
current position is not included in the CRB's conflict-of-interest code, pursuant to the
requirements of Section 87302, the one-year ban period applicable to Mr. Zimmerman and his
involvement before the cRB has most likely not begun to run because it appears he continues to
hold a pos^ition at the CRB that entails the making oi participation in the making of governmental
decisions.2 (Section 87406(dXl); Regulatio n 18746.1(a)(2); (Jnterreiner Advice Letter, No. I-9g-
299.) 
I
2 You should contact your code reviewing body or the Commission's Technical Assistance Division to
assist you in determining whether Mr. Zimmerman is property included in or excluded fiom the agency,s contlict of
interest code. Determination of this issue will also determine what Mr. Zimmerman,s ,"rponrilirities are with
respect to filing Form 700s.
If Mr' Zimmerman is placed in a position at CRB that does not involve any of the above
described actions, and thus should not be designated in the CRB conflict-of-interest code, the
one-year period would begin once he left his designated position and would not be extended as a
result of having served in the non-designated posiiion.
section 87406 prohibits specified officials from acting as agents or attomeys or otherwise
representing, for compensation, "any other person, by making ony lormal or informal
appearance, or by making any oral or written communication, beiore any state administrative
agency, or officer or employee thereof," for one year after the official leit the agency,s
employment "if the appearance or communicatiin is made for the purpose of inJlttencing
adrninistrative or legislative action, or inflttencing any action or proceeding involving the
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocatton oj o plrmit, license, grant, or contract, or the
sale.or purchase of goodl or propertyi' (Emptrasis aaoeo.j rn" ooJ-y"* period for the ban
begins to run once the official has left his designated position with the agency.
Regulation 187 46.2(a) further provides :
"(a) For purposes of Govemment Code Section 87406,a formal or informal
appearance or oral or written communication is for the pu{pose of influencing if it
is made for the principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing,
modifying, opposing, deraying, or advincing the action oiproceedid:l"
appearance or communication includes, but is not limited to, converJing by
telephone or in pers-on, coresponding with in writing or by electronic
transmission, attending a meeting, and delivering oriending any communication.,,
Regulation 18746.1(b) outlines the circumstances when the prohibitions of the one-year
ban will apply' under this regulation, an official covered by the one-year ban is prohibited from
making an appearance or communication if all of the foilowing apply:
"(1) The official has left his or her state office or employment, which means he or
the ha,s either permanently reft state service or is o., u l.ur" of absence.
"(2)The appearance or cofitmunication is made within 12 months after leaving
state office or employment.
"(3) The public official is compensated, or promised compensation, for the
appearance or communication. However, a payment made for necessary travel,
meals, and accommodations received directiyin connection with voluniary
services is not prohibited or limited by this slction.
"(4) The appearance or communication is made on behalf of any person as an
agent, attomey, or representative of that person. An appearance or communication
made by a public officiar solely to represent his or hei personal interests, as
defined in2 cal. code Regs., Section rg702.4, subdivision (bxl), is not
prohibited or limited by this section.
"(5) The appearance or communication is made for the purpose of influencing, as
defined in2 Cal. Code Regs. Section 18746.2, any legisiative or administrative
action, or any discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or
revocation of a permit, license, grant or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods
or property.
"(A) Services performed to administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements of
an existing P,ermig license, grant, contract, or sale agreement may be excluded
from the prohibitions of this regulation, provided the services do not involve the
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of any of these actions or
proceedings . . ..
"(6) The appearance or communication is made before any officer or employee of
any of the following:
"(A) Any state administrative agency that the public official worked for or
represented during the 12 months before leaving state office or employment . . ..
"(B) Any state administrative agency which budget, personnel, und oti".
operations are subject to the direction and control of any agency described in
subdivision (bX6XA) . . ..
"(C) Any state administrative agency subject to the direction and control of the
Govemor, if the official was a designated employee of the Governor's office
during the Lz months before leaving state office tr employment.,, 3
Communications restricted by the one-year ban include any formal or informal
appearance or oral or written communication made to influence legislative or administrative
action or any action on a proceeding. (Section 87406(d)(l).) These communications include,
but are not limited to, conversing directly or by telephone,'corresponding by writing or e-mail,
attending a meeting, and delivering or sending uny "o-*unication. 6elguiation lg746.Z(a).)
A communication is considered to be for the purpose of influencing legiJative or administrative
action "if it is made for the principal purpose of iupporting, pro*oiingl influencing, modifying,
opposing, delaying, or advancing the action or proieedin*"4 lReguliiion 1g746.2(a).)
Certain communications are not rcstricted under the one-year ban. A communication is
not subject to the one-year ban when the former official:
3 Regulation I 8746' I (b)( I ) refers to an official leaving a state position that is designated in the agency,s
confl ict-of-interest code.
a "Legislative action" is defined at Section 82Q37 tomean "the drafting, introduction, consideration, r
modification, enactment or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other matter by the
Legislature or by either house or any committee, subcommittee, jointor select committee thereof, or by a member or
employee of the Legislature acting in his official capacity. "Legislative action" also means the action of the
Governor in approving or vetoing any bill." Section gZmztr,) -provides 
that ..'Administrative action, means the
proposal, drafting, development, consideration, amendment, enictment, or defeat by any state agency of any rule,
regulation, or other action in any ratemaking proceeding or any quasi-legislatir" pro...airg, *rii.r, shall include any
proceeding governed by Chapter 3.5 (commencing with- Section i t:+o)'of part I of Division 3 of Title 2.
"(1) Participates as a panelist or formal speaker at a conference or similar public
event for educational purposes or to disseminate research and the subject matter
does not pertain to a specific action or proceeding;
"(2) Attends a general informational meeting, seminar, or similar event;
"(3) Requests information concerning any matter of public record; or
"(4) Communicates with the press." (Regulation rg746.2(b)(l)-(4).)
Thus, the one-year ban would not prevent Mr. Zimmerman from requesting information
generally available to the public about agency business from the CRB or otirer state agencies
while working at the CRJPA. Nor would it prohibit him from attending iJo.-otioral meetings
regarding existing laws, regulations, or polic^ies, so long as he does ,ot"att"*ft to influence

having social conversations with "-pioy.., of the cRB that are not aimed at influencing its
administrative or legislative actions ire not prohibited by the ban. (Tobias Advice Letter, No.
A-96-089.) (Section 87406(dXl).)
Whether a particular meeting or conversation is for the purpose of influencing
administrative or legislative action or a.specific proceeding (as defined by Regul ation 1g746.2)
depends on the facts of each case. For instanc",lf u, ex-designated employee attends a public
meeting with many other persons, where- there- are many topics on the agenda, it may be
reasonable to infer that the ex-designated employ""', uit"ndance is not ior the purpose of
influencing the agency's action. conversely, *h"r" there is a small meeting to discuss a
particular administrative or legislative action, or _other specific action o. pro-""."oing involving the
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of u p".-it, license, grant, or contract, or the sale
or purchase of goods or property (section 87406(d)i it may more readily be inferred that the
former designated employee's presence at the -""tirg is intended to influence agency action.
(Ramirez Advice Letter, No. 4-99-300.)5
Similarly, as noted ab9ve, Regulation 18746.1(b)(5)(A) provides that performing services
to administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements oi an existing permit, license, grant,
contract, or sale agreement are not considered appearances or communications prohibited under
Section 87406, so long as Mr. zimmerman does not influence rril;;;i;riJr" 
or legislarive
action, or the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, Iicense, grant, or contract,
or the sale or purchase of goods or property.
I
Accordingly, for purposes of the one-year ban under Section g74}6,the Act does not
prevent Mr' Zimmerman from accepting post-govemmental employment in the private sector or
a local government agency such as trre cvwo, but for twelve months after he leaves a

designated position or position that should be designated at the cRB, he may not make any

awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or coitract, or the sale or purchase of goods
or property.
The Permanent Ban on.,switching SidesD
The Act's permanent.oan prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides,, and
participating, for compensation, in a specific proceeding involving the State of Califomia, or
assisting others in the proceeding, if the proceeding is o-ne in which the former state employee
participated while employed by the stare. (See secJions g74ol-g7402; Regulation 1g741.1.)
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban applicable to any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other
proceeding in which Mr. Zimmerman participatea while empl,oyed ur.u rtut.-udministrative
official' A State administrative official--.*r o'every member, 6ffi""., "*pioy"" of a state
administrative agency who as part of his or her official responsibilities engages in any judicial,
quasi-judicial or oth^er ptoceedings in other than a purely clerical, secretarial or ministerial
capacity." (Section 87400(b).)
"'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding, application, request
for a ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge,
accusation, arrest or other particular matter involving a specifil purty oipurties in any court or
state administrarive agency. . .." (Section g7400(c)J en official has ..p;;;i;ated,,in a 
..
proceeding if he or she took part in the proceeding ;'personally, and substantia'ily through
decision, approval, disapproval, formal written reiomm"ndation, renaerinfaovice on a
substantial basis, investigation, or use of confidential information . . ..,, (Section g7400(d).)
The permanent ban does not apply to a "new" proceeding even in cases where the new
proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior pro"""ding in which the official had participated.
A "new" proceeding not subjectto the permanent ban typlcally involves different parties, subject
matter, or circumstances that differ from those involved i, p..uiou, proceedints. (rtlsl Advice
Letter, No' A-04-187; also see Donovan Advice Letter, No. t-or-t te.; New clntracts let by the
employee's former agency, in which the former employee did not paiicipate, are consioered new
proceedings. (Leslie Advice Letter, No. I-89-649.) A lnew" contract is one based on new
consideration and new terms, even if it involves the same parties. (FerberAdvice Letter, No.
r-99-104: Anderson Advice Letter, No. A-98-159.) In addition, the application, drafting, and the
award of a contract, license, or approval is considered a proceeding seiarate from the monitoring
and performance of the contract, license, or approval. (Anderson, supra; BlonienAdvice Letter,
No. 4-89-463.)

CONCLUSION: l' No. If Gerald Zimmerman works in a position with the Colorado River Board that is
not and should not be designated in the agency's ionflicrof-[nterest code, he will not be further
restricted by the Act's one-year revolving cloor ban as o ,"r,-,lt of having worked in that position.

ret'erences are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair political practices
Commission are contained in sections l8l l0 through 18997 of Title 2 t>f tieCalitbrnia code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Califbrnia Code of Regulations, unless orherwise indicated.
2' Yes, Gerald Zimmerman may work in an undesignated position at the Colorado River

Granito Redwine and Sherrill 1950 Market Street Riverside, California 92501- 17 ZO Your Request for Advice Our File No. 4-10-187 Dear Mr. Granito: This letter responds to your reqlrest for advice on behalf of the Coachella Valley Water District' the Colorado River Joint Powers Authority, and Gerald Zimmerman regarding the post governmental employment provisions of the political Reform Act (the,.Act,,).r QT,IESTIONS l' will the former Executive Director of the Colorado River Board, Gerald Zimmerman, who has taken a new undesignated position with the Colorado River Board, be subject to an additional one-year revolving door ban after he leaves his employment in the undesignated position? 2' May Gerald Zimmetman work in an undesignated position at the Colorado River Board and also work as an independent contractor for ihe colbrado River Joint powers Authority simultaneously if he does not engage in any activities that are prohibited by the Act,s one-year revolving door ban? CONCLUSIONS l' No. If Gerald Zimmerman works in a position with the Colorado River Board that is not and should not be designated in the agency's ionflicrof-[nterest code, he will not be further restricted by the Act's one-year revolving cloor ban as o ,"r,-,lt of having worked in that position. He may not engage in any activities that are prohibited by the Act,s one-year revolving door ban or Section 87100 while working at the Colorado River Joint powers Authority. FACTS You are the General Counsel for the Coachella Valley Water District (..CVWD,,). The cvwD is a member of the colorado River Joint Powers Autiiority ("CRJpA';;, *hi"h consists exclusively of six local member agencies. All of the CRJPA agencies have a representative seat on the Colorado River Board of Califomia's ("CRB") l0-person goveming board. The CRB is a state agency. The CVWD, CRJPA and the CRB entered into a Joint poweis Agreement, dated May 13, 7947, for the purpose of jointly sponsoring, encouraging and supporti;g a separate, independent unincorporated association of citizensif Southern Califomia referred to as the Colorado River Association. You have been authorized by the CVWD, CRJPA and Gerald Zimmerman to seek advice on their behalf. Gerald zimmerman is the former Executive Director of the CRB.
